title: Variables and controls: what a comparison really tests

objectives.statement: Identify the independent, dependent, and controlled variables in a supplied comparison and explain the job of each.
Distinguish a controlled variable from a control condition and identify a confounder that changes with the independent variable and could affect the dependent variable.
Identify positive-control and negative-control conditions and use their results to decide whether an unknown result is interpretable.

scenes.title: When nothing happened has two meanings
scenes.contentHtml:
When nothing happened has two meanings

A small fluorescence reader displays 0 for an unknown sample. One explanation is that the sample contains none of the target. Another is that the indicator failed, the reader was mis-set, or the reaction never had a chance to work. The same zero can support very different stories.

To tell those stories apart, you need more than a prediction. You need to know what was deliberately changed, what was recorded, what should stay alike, what else might be changing with the comparison, and which extra test conditions show that the setup can produce both an expected signal and an expected baseline.

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to name those jobs and use them to decide what a comparison actually tests. The goal is not to make an experiment “perfect.” It is to make the intended comparison and its remaining limits visible.

Retrieve the chain you already built

This lesson follows From observation to question (PREM-SCI-001) and Hypotheses and predictions (planned PREM-SCI-002; outcome PREM-02.02). The second topic does not yet have a published open lesson, so the recovery step below is included here rather than sending you to a missing page.

Before reading on, reconstruct the chain from those two prior topics:

Check
id check-prerequisite-chain

observation → bounded empirical question → ___ → conditions → ___

Fill both blanks from memory, then add one sentence explaining what the prediction contributes that the hypothesis does not.

A useful completion is hypothesis → prediction. A question says what you want to find out; a hypothesis proposes an answer or relationship; and a prediction names the record expected if the hypothesis holds under stated conditions.

If that distinction was not available from memory, pause and make one small example: write a proposed relationship as your hypothesis, then write the result you would expect to record under one stated set of conditions as your prediction. Continue only when the second sentence names an observable record rather than repeating the proposed relationship.

Variables and controls turn those stated conditions into an inspectable comparison. They show which link the record can test.

Six questions, six jobs

Suppose a fictional indicator should fluoresce when a target molecule is present. A team compares a low target concentration with a high target concentration and records fluorescence after ten minutes.

Ask these questions in order:

Ordinary-language question Technical name
What factor did the team deliberately change? Independent variable
What response did the team record? Dependent variable
Which other relevant conditions did the team deliberately keep alike? Controlled variables
What other factor changed with the independent variable and could also change the response? Confounder
Which condition should show little or no tested response? Negative control
Which condition should produce a known response if the setup is working? Positive control

In this comparison, target concentration is the independent variable because the team intentionally sets it to different levels. Fluorescence after ten minutes is the dependent variable because it is the recorded response.

Sample volume, temperature, incubation time, and reader setting are controlled variables when the team deliberately keeps them alike across the target-concentration groups. A controlled variable is a condition, such as temperature. It is not the same thing as a control condition or control group, which is an extra comparison included for a particular purpose.

What if every low-concentration sample uses reagent lot A in the morning, while every high-concentration sample uses reagent lot B in the afternoon? Reagent lot now travels with target concentration. If reagent lot can also change fluorescence, it is a confounder: the fluorescence difference could reflect concentration, reagent lot, or both. The problem is not merely that reagent lot varies. It varies in step with the independent variable and could affect the dependent variable.

Two extra conditions make the zero reading more informative:

  • A negative control contains no target and is expected to stay near the baseline. If it fluoresces strongly, background signal or contamination may be present.
  • A positive control contains a known target condition that is expected to fluoresce. If it stays near zero, the run has not shown that the setup can reveal a signal.

The negative control asks, “Does the setup produce a signal when it should not?” The positive control asks, “Can the setup produce the expected signal when it should?” These names describe expected response roles, not whether the result is good or bad.

Controls expose particular failures and rival explanations. They do not erase measurement error, sampling limits, or every unmeasured influence. The next scene shows how the six jobs work together in one complete audit.

Source Note
claims claim-variable-roles, claim-confounder-ambiguity, claim-control-functions, claim-controls-have-limitssources source-nist-handbook, source-ncbi-assay-guidelines

The NIST handbook and the NCBI-hosted assay guidance support the factor/response distinction, the need to manage other influential variables, and the comparison and quality-check functions of controls. Terminology and concrete control materials vary across fields; the fictional indicator, six-question table, and wording are original teaching material.

scenes.title: Audit one comparison from start to finish
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit one comparison from start to finish

The following indicator run, settings, and results are fictional. You do not need laboratory access, chemicals, or personal experience with fluorescence. The task is to determine what the comparison can mean.

Worked Example
id worked-example-indicator-auditlabel A complete six-role audit

1. State the task.

A team wants to test this prediction: under the stated indicator conditions, samples set to 8 target units will produce higher fluorescence than samples set to 2 target units. Identify the six variable and control roles, find any ambiguity, and decide whether an unknown zero reading can be interpreted.

2. Represent the givens.

Part of the run Stated condition
Test comparison 2 or 8 target units
Recorded outcome fluorescence units after 10 minutes
Shared settings 1 mL volume, 25 °C, 10 minutes, reader setting R
Scheduling every 2-unit sample uses lot A in the morning; every 8-unit sample uses lot B in the afternoon
No-target condition expected to remain near baseline
Known-target condition expected to fluoresce clearly

The unknowns are not numerical. We need to determine which job each part performs and whether another factor is locked to the test comparison.

3. Choose a plan.

First locate what the team set differently and what it recorded. Next separate held-alike conditions from extra control conditions. Then search for any factor that moves with the intended change and could also influence the response. Finally, interpret the controls before interpreting the unknown.

4. Execute the role map.

  • Independent variable: target concentration, set to 2 or 8 units.
  • Dependent variable: fluorescence recorded after 10 minutes.
  • Controlled variables: sample volume, temperature, incubation time, and reader setting, because the team holds them alike across the concentration comparison.
  • Negative control: the no-target condition, expected to show the baseline.
  • Positive control: the known-target condition, expected to show that the system can produce a clear response.

Reagent lot is a confounder because lot A appears only with 2 target units and lot B only with 8 target units, while lot could influence fluorescence. Time of day is another candidate confounder for the same reason. If the 8-unit samples fluoresce more, the record cannot separate a concentration effect from a lot or time effect.

5. Repair the comparison.

Run both target concentrations across both reagent lots and both time periods instead of pairing one concentration with one lot and time. Keep volume, temperature, incubation time, and reader setting alike. This breaks the fixed link between target concentration and the rival factors. It does not promise that every other influence has vanished.

6. Check the controls before the unknown.

Imagine this result pattern:

Condition Fictional fluorescence result
Negative control 1
Positive control 1
Unknown 0

The negative control is near baseline, but the positive control also failed to show the known response. Therefore, the run has not demonstrated that it can detect the target. The unknown zero is not evidence that the target is absent; repair and rerun the system before interpreting it.

As a second check, suppose the negative control reads 70 while the positive control reads 74. The system can display a high number, but the high background means the expected signal is not separated from the baseline. Again, the unknown would not support the intended conclusion.

7. Interpret the bounded result.

If the confounding is repaired, the negative control stays near baseline, the positive control shows the expected response, and the 8-unit samples consistently exceed the 2-unit samples, the record supports a concentration-related difference under these fictional conditions. It does not establish how the indicator works, guarantee the same result in another setting, or eliminate every remaining source of uncertainty.

8. Explain the decisive decision.

Before continuing, answer: why must the positive-control result be inspected before treating an unknown zero as “no target”?

A defensible answer is that a zero has two live explanations until the setup demonstrates that it can show the expected response. The positive control tests that capability. If you answered only “because controls are important,” make the two competing explanations explicit.

A compact audit you can reuse

Check
id check-compact-audit

From memory, complete this sequence before opening the feedback:

changed → recorded → held alike → ___ → expected baseline → expected response

Feedback after your attempt

The missing step is changed alongside and could affect the record—the confounder check. The whole sequence is a practical audit, not a universal recipe for choosing a study design.

Source Note
claims claim-variable-roles, claim-confounder-ambiguity, claim-control-functions, claim-controls-have-limitssources source-nist-handbook, source-ncbi-assay-guidelines

The sources support treating manipulated factors and recorded responses as different roles, addressing extraneous factors through design, and using negative and positive controls as comparison and performance checks. The complete indicator case, target units, result values, audit sequence, and repairs are independently created fictional material.

scenes.title: Find the role, then find the flaw
scenes.contentHtml:
Find the role, then find the flaw

Begin without looking back. Write six short questions that recover the six jobs. Put the technical name beside each only after you have described its job in ordinary language.

A useful check is:

changed; recorded; held alike; changed alongside and able to affect the record; expected baseline; expected known response

If held alike and expected baseline sound identical, keep going: the first describes a condition maintained across the main groups; the second describes an extra comparison whose expected result helps interpret the system.

One part supplied

A fictional materials team compares two curing temperatures for the same resin. It records the force needed to break each cured strip. Resin volume, mould shape, curing duration, and testing machine are kept alike. The lower-temperature strips are all made by technician K and the higher-temperature strips by technician M.

Check
id check-resin-role-map

Complete the role map before opening the feedback:

Role Part of the comparison
Independent variable ___
Dependent variable ___
Two controlled variables ___
Candidate confounder ___

Feedback after your attempt

A defensible map identifies curing temperature as the independent variable, breaking force as the dependent variable, any two of resin volume, mould shape, curing duration, and testing machine as controlled variables, and technician as a candidate confounder. Technician qualifies only if technician differences could affect breaking force; the fixed pairing with temperature creates the ambiguity.

To repair the pairing, have both technicians prepare strips at both temperatures under the same stated conditions. If technician cannot affect the response, it is not a confounder even though it differs. The label follows the relationship, not a memorized list of suspicious factors.

Less support: a condition is not a group

A fictional seedling comparison gives one group nutrient solution and another group the same volume of water. It records stem-height change over seven days. Both groups use the same seed variety, soil mass, pot size, light schedule, and liquid volume.

Check
id check-seedling-control-condition

Answer before opening the feedback:

  1. Which factor is the independent variable?
  2. Which record is the dependent variable?
  3. Name two controlled variables.
  4. Is the water-only group a controlled variable or a control condition? Explain.

Feedback after your attempt

The independent variable is liquid treatment: nutrient solution or water. The dependent variable is seven-day stem-height change. Seed variety, soil mass, pot size, light schedule, and liquid volume are controlled variables. The water-only group is a control condition because it supplies a comparison level; it is not a condition held at one value across both groups.

Whether that water-only group should be called a negative control depends on the prediction and expected response. If it is expected to lack the nutrient-associated response and provide the baseline, it performs a negative-control role. “Control group” alone does not tell you what every control is designed to reveal.

Misconception
id misconception-any-uncontrolled-confounds

Commit first. A different desk position is chosen at random for every strip in a materials test. Is desk position automatically a confounder because it was not held constant?

Tempting shortcut: every uncontrolled variable is a confounder.

Why the shortcut fails: a confounder must create a rival explanation for the comparison. It must vary systematically with the independent variable and be able to affect the dependent variable. Random desk positions that occur across both curing temperatures are not automatically confounded with temperature.

Repair: ask two questions: “Does this factor track the independent variable?” and “Could it affect the recorded response?” Both must be plausible for the factor to confound this comparison.

Recheck: all 20 °C strips are tested on Monday and all 30 °C strips on Friday after the machine was recalibrated. Day or calibration state could affect breaking force and is locked to temperature. That is a candidate confounder.

Independent mixed practice

For each case, identify the requested roles before reading the feedback.

Case A: background where there should be none. A fictional image detector should highlight cracks. A blank, crack-free reference image is the negative control; a reference image with a known crack is the positive control. The blank image is highlighted, and the known-crack image is also highlighted.

Which control failed, and what should happen next?

The negative control failed because the detector produced the tested signal where the baseline was expected. The positive control alone does not rescue the run: investigate the background or false-positive behavior before interpreting unknown images.

Case B: no response where one should appear. The same detector leaves both the blank reference and known-crack reference unmarked.

The positive control failed. The blank result is expected for the negative control, but the detector has not shown that it can reveal a crack. Repair the detection path before treating unmarked unknown images as crack-free.

Case C: complete an independent audit. Choose one of these supplied fictional comparisons. No data collection or equipment is required.

  • Package seals: A team compares sealant A with sealant B and records the force needed to open each sealed package. Package material, seal length, curing time, and test speed are held alike. Every sealant A package is tested on machine 1 and every sealant B package on machine 2. An unsealed package should require almost no opening force; a certified reference seal should open within a known force range.
  • Motion detection: A team compares detector algorithm A with algorithm B and records an alert score for supplied video clips. Clip length, resolution, frame rate, and decision threshold are held alike. Algorithm A always runs on computer 1 and algorithm B on computer 2. An empty-corridor clip should produce a near-baseline alert score; a reference clip with a known moving object should produce a score above the stated detection threshold.
Check
id check-independent-six-role-audit

For your chosen comparison, name the independent and dependent variables, at least two controlled variables, the candidate confounder, and the negative- and positive-control conditions. Then explain both links that make the extra factor a candidate confounder, what a failed negative control would show, and what a failed positive control would prevent you from concluding about an unknown.

Self-check after your attempt

Do not check the nouns alone. Your audit is defensible only if every relationship below is present:

  1. The independent variable is the factor deliberately set to two levels, and the dependent variable is the response recorded.
  2. Each controlled variable you named is explicitly held alike across those levels.
  3. The candidate confounder is paired with the independent variable and could change the dependent variable.
  4. The negative control is the condition expected to stay near baseline; a response there exposes background or a response where none is expected.
  5. The positive control is the condition expected to produce a known response; failure there means the system has not shown that it can reveal the response, so a matching unknown cannot yet be interpreted.

If one relationship is missing, revise that row and repeat the audit on the other supplied comparison. Different fields use different control forms, so use the expected response—not the label alone—to assign each control role.

Source Note
claims claim-variable-roles, claim-confounder-ambiguity, claim-control-functions, claim-controls-have-limitssources source-nist-handbook, source-ncbi-assay-guidelines

The sources support the manipulated-factor, response, extraneous-variable, and control roles used in these discrimination tasks. They also support holding some influential conditions constant while recognizing the scope limits of that choice. All resin, seedling, image-detector, package-seal, and motion-detector cases are original fictional examples.

scenes.title: Transfer the audit to a field sensor
scenes.contentHtml:
Transfer the audit to a field sensor

A fictional field team is evaluating two filter cartridges for a nitrate sensor used in a stream-monitoring project. No water is collected and no sensor is operated for this lesson.

The team fits either cartridge A or cartridge B, exposes the sensor to the same supplied nitrate standard, and records the sensor reading after five minutes. It keeps standard volume, stated nitrate concentration, sensor model, exposure time, and housing temperature alike. Every cartridge A run uses an older battery pack; every cartridge B run uses a new battery pack.

The team also records:

  • a nitrate-free standard that should produce a near-baseline reading; and
  • a second certified nitrate standard that should produce a response within a stated range.
Check
id check-field-sensor-transfer

Before opening the comparison, make a six-row audit. Identify each role, then answer these questions:

  1. What ambiguity does the battery pairing create?
  2. How would you break that pairing without changing the cartridge question?
  3. If the certified nitrate standard produces a near-zero reading, what can you conclude about an unknown near-zero reading?
  4. If the nitrate-free standard produces a large reading, what problem becomes visible?

Compare your audit

  • Independent variable: cartridge type, A or B.
  • Dependent variable: sensor reading after five minutes.
  • Controlled variables: standard volume, nitrate concentration, sensor model, exposure time, and housing temperature.
  • Confounder: battery age, because old batteries occur only with cartridge A and new batteries only with cartridge B, while battery condition could change the sensor reading.
  • Negative control: the nitrate-free standard, expected to show the baseline.
  • Positive control: the certified nitrate standard expected to produce a response in the stated range.

Use old and new battery packs with both cartridge types, or otherwise arrange battery condition so it is not fixed to cartridge type. This makes cartridge and battery explanations separable while preserving the intended comparison.

If the positive control is near zero, the system has not demonstrated that it can reveal a nitrate response, so an unknown near-zero reading is not interpretable as “no nitrate.” If the negative control gives a large reading, background signal, contamination, or another failure is visible; unknown high readings should not be trusted until that problem is resolved.

This transfer does not ask you to choose a field-study design or make a causal claim about real streams. The next outcome distinguishes experimental from observational designs. Here, the task is narrower: identify the jobs inside a supplied comparison and explain which interpretations those jobs permit.

Carry the whole chain forward

Check
id check-cumulative-inquiry-chain

Complete this sequence from memory before opening the suggested ending:

observation → bounded empirical question → hypothesis → conditions → prediction → ___

Suggested ending after your attempt

One useful ending is:

role audit: changed, recorded, held alike, confounder check, negative control, positive control

The first lesson separated observations from explanations and built answerable questions. The second turned hypotheses into predictions. This lesson asks whether the comparison distinguishes the prediction from a failed system or a rival factor.

Mastery check

The assessment contains four untimed, retakeable items worth twelve points. Attempt every item before opening its answer and reasoning. Mastery means earning all twelve points: three for identifying the independent, dependent, and controlled roles and explaining each job; three for distinguishing a controlled variable, control condition, and confounder; two for interpreting positive and negative controls; and four for an integrated transfer audit in a case not answered in the lesson. If you miss a criterion, follow its feedback and retry with the alternate context rather than copying the original answer.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every task can be completed in prose, bullets, or a table. You may type, dictate into your own device, or use assistive technology; no audio or image upload is required. All table relationships are also explained in nearby prose. The lesson does not depend on color, visual position, dragging, sound, speed, wet-laboratory work, field sampling, or access to equipment. The cases contain no real participant, patient, or environmental data.

Recovery route

If you could not reconstruct hypothesis → prediction, return to Hypotheses and predictions (planned PREM-SCI-002; outcome PREM-02.02). Because that topic does not yet have a published open lesson, use the recovery check in the first scene: write one proposed relationship, then a separate observable record expected under stated conditions. Seek your course text or instructor support before continuing if those two statements still collapse into one.

If independent and dependent variables blur together, write set differently → record response and place one case detail over each phrase.

If a controlled variable looks like a control group, ask whether the feature is held at one value across the main groups or supplied as an extra comparison. Held alike means controlled variable; extra comparison means control condition.

If every nuisance factor looks like a confounder, apply both tests: it must track the independent variable, and it must be able to affect the dependent variable.

If positive and negative controls reverse, ignore the names at first. Ask should respond? for the positive control and should stay at baseline? for the negative control.

If a control fails, stop interpreting the unknown. State which capability or background check failed, repair that part, and use a new attempt.

Source Note
claims claim-variable-roles, claim-confounder-ambiguity, claim-control-functions, claim-controls-have-limitssources source-nist-handbook, source-ncbi-assay-guidelines

The sources support the role audit and the limited conclusions drawn when a comparison factor, extraneous influence, or control result is unresolved. The sensor case, battery pairing, retrieval chain, and feedback are independently created fictional teaching material.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable for this lesson. Material authoring assistance used OpenAI Codex, model GPT-5, in isolated run 5619914e-ad81-4db9-8e6e-928a2f71146c on 2026-07-20. The one-pass final lesson revision used OpenAI Codex, model GPT-5, in fresh run 6a4a4597-b6c3-4f24-8b86-3bcd59d166ed on 2026-07-20; its exact provenance and material-instructions digest are recorded in adjudication.json. Instruction digests cover the disclosed UTF-8 material task payloads including their final newlines and exclude hidden provider and system instructions.

items.prompt: A fictional electronics team sets a fan to 1000 or 2000 revolutions per minute, records component temperature after 15 minutes, and keeps the enclosure, room temperature, component model, and temperature sensor alike. Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, and controlled variables. For each role, explain its job in this comparison using what the team set, recorded, or held alike.

answer.correct: Fan speed is the independent variable because the team deliberately sets it to two levels. Component temperature after 15 minutes is the dependent variable because it is the recorded response. The enclosure, room temperature, component model, and temperature sensor are controlled variables because the team holds them alike across the fan-speed comparison.
answer.summary: The independent variable is set differently, the dependent variable is recorded, and the controlled variables are held alike.
answer.reasoning: Award one point for each role only when the correct case detail and its job are both present: fan speed is deliberately set differently; component temperature after 15 minutes is recorded as the response; and the enclosure, room temperature, component model, and temperature sensor are held alike across the main comparison. If you reversed independent and dependent, use the verbs set and record. On a retry, map the heater setting, final water temperature, and a shared cup type, water volume, starting temperature, and timer, again stating what job each role performs.
acceptableResponses: Any response that identifies all three roles and explains them with the set differently, recorded response, and held alike relationships.
commonErrors: Calling the recorded response the independent variable
Calling a measurement the controlled variable merely because the team measures it
Omitting the conditions deliberately held alike

items.prompt: In a fictional seven-day seedling comparison, one group receives nutrient solution and one receives the same volume of water. Stem-height change is recorded. Seed variety, soil mass, pot size, and light duration are held alike, but every nutrient tray is on a brighter upper shelf and every water tray is on a dimmer lower shelf. Identify one controlled variable, identify the water-only condition's role, identify the confounder, and explain why that factor confounds the nutrient comparison.

answer.correct: A complete response names seed variety, soil mass, pot size, or light duration as a controlled variable; identifies the water-only group as the control condition and, under the stated nutrient-response prediction, the negative control; and identifies shelf brightness or shelf position as a confounder because brightness changes with treatment and could also change stem-height growth.
answer.summary: A held-alike condition, an extra comparison condition, and a confounder perform different jobs.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for naming a condition genuinely held alike, one for identifying water-only as the control condition or negative-control condition rather than a controlled variable, and one for identifying brightness or shelf position and stating both links: it is fixed to liquid treatment and could affect stem-height change. All three are required for 3/3. If you only wrote that the shelf was uncontrolled, add why it tracks the independent variable and could influence the dependent variable. On a retry, compare detergent A with detergent B while recording stain removal: fabric, stain, load mass, water temperature, and wash time are held alike; a water-only wash is an extra comparison; and detergent A always runs in machine 1 while detergent B always runs in machine 2. Re-identify the same three roles and both confounder links.
acceptableResponses: Any response that correctly distinguishes one held-alike condition from the water control and gives the two-link reason that shelf brightness confounds treatment with growth.
commonErrors: Calling the water-only group a controlled variable
Calling every varying feature a confounder without connecting it to treatment and response
Naming nutrient solution as the confounder even though it is the intended independent variable

items.prompt: A fictional detector gives a near-zero reading for a no-target negative control, a near-zero reading for a known-target positive control, and a near-zero reading for an unknown. Explain what each control result shows and whether the unknown can be interpreted as lacking the target.

answer.correct: The near-zero negative control is the expected baseline. The known-target positive control failed because it did not produce the expected response, so the detector has not shown that it can reveal the target. The unknown near-zero reading therefore cannot yet be interpreted as target absent; repair and retry the detection path first.
answer.summary: The baseline behaved as expected, but the known-response condition did not show that the detector can reveal a signal.
answer.reasoning: Award one point for recognizing that the near-zero negative control is the expected baseline and one for recognizing that the near-zero positive control failed and blocks a no-target interpretation of the unknown. Repair the detection path and retry before interpreting the unknown. On a retry, use this unambiguous high-background pattern: the negative control, positive control, and unknown all read high. State what the high negative control shows about background and whether the unknown can be interpreted; this re-tests the negative-baseline and unknown-interpretation criterion.
acceptableResponses: Any response that recognizes the expected negative-control baseline, diagnoses the failed positive control, and withholds a no-target interpretation for the unknown.
commonErrors: Treating a matching unknown and negative control as sufficient when the positive control failed
Assuming positive and negative describe whether a result is desirable
Ignoring control results once an unknown value is available

items.prompt: Audit a fictional acoustics comparison not used in the lesson examples. A team compares sound-dampening panel A with panel B and records the sound level behind each panel after 30 seconds. Speaker tone, starting volume, room, speaker-to-panel distance, panel dimensions, and recording duration are held alike. Every panel A run uses microphone X, while every panel B run uses microphone Y. A silent-input recording should remain near baseline; a supplied calibration tone should produce a reading within a known range. Identify the independent and dependent variables, two controlled variables, the confounder with its two-link justification, and the positive and negative controls. Then state what a near-zero calibration-tone reading means for an unknown near-zero sound-level reading.

answer.correct: A complete audit identifies panel type as the independent variable and the sound level recorded after 30 seconds as the dependent variable; names any two of speaker tone, starting volume, room, speaker-to-panel distance, panel dimensions, or recording duration as controlled variables; identifies microphone identity as a confounder because microphone X is fixed to panel A and microphone Y to panel B while microphone differences could affect the recorded sound level; identifies the silent-input recording as the negative control and the calibration tone as the positive control; and explains that a near-zero positive-control result means the system has not demonstrated response capability, so an unknown near-zero reading cannot be interpreted as a very low sound level.
answer.summary: The six-role audit reveals the intended panel comparison, the microphone ambiguity, and the failed capability check.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for the correct independent/dependent pair, one for two valid controlled variables, one for microphone identity plus both confounding links, and one for both control labels plus the failed-positive-control interpretation. All four are required for 4/4. If you listed roles without reasons, add the verbs: set, record, hold alike, travels with and can affect, should stay at baseline, and should respond. On a retry, audit two fictional water-test strips when strip A is always read with phone camera 1 and strip B with phone camera 2; a blank solution and a supplied known-concentration standard serve as controls. Reconstruct every role rather than copying the first answer.
acceptableResponses: Any response that maps all six roles equivalently, states both links that make microphone identity a confounder, and withholds interpretation after the positive control fails.
commonErrors: Calling microphone identity a controlled variable even though it is fixed to panel type
Reversing the silent-input and calibration-tone control roles
Concluding that the unknown has a very low sound level when the positive control is near zero
Naming the recorded sound level as the independent variable

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when fan speed is identified as the independent variable and explained as the factor deliberately set to two levels.
Award 1 point only when component temperature after 15 minutes is identified as the dependent variable and explained as the recorded response.
Award 1 point only when the enclosure, room temperature, component model, and sensor are identified as controlled variables and explained as conditions held alike across fan-speed levels.

rubrics.title: Controlled condition, control condition, and confounder

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when seed variety, soil mass, pot size, or light duration is correctly named as a controlled variable.
Award 1 point only when the water-only group is identified as a control condition or negative-control condition rather than a controlled variable.
Award 1 point only when shelf brightness or position is identified and both links are stated: it tracks liquid treatment and could affect stem-height change.
Award 1 point only when the near-zero negative control is recognized as the expected baseline result.
Award 1 point only when the near-zero positive control is identified as a failure that blocks a no-target interpretation of the unknown.
Award 1 point only when panel type is independent and the sound level after 30 seconds is dependent.
Award 1 point only when at least two valid held-alike conditions are identified.
Award 1 point only when microphone identity is identified and both its fixed pairing with panel type and possible effect on recorded sound level are stated.

Award 1 point only when silent input is labelled negative, the calibration tone is labelled positive, and a near-zero positive control is used to withhold a very-low-level interpretation of a near-zero unknown.

sources.title: NIST/SEMATECH e-Handbook of Statistical Methods
creators: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify facts about experimental factors, responses, controlled and uncontrolled inputs, and confounding; no wording, examples, tables, or figures were copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The accountable contributor checked the NIST copyright and disclaimer route before substantive access. It presents NIST web pages as a public service and states that unmarked information is public information; no term prohibiting this limited agent reading was found.

sources.title: In Vivo Assay Guidelines
sources.publisher: Eli Lilly & Company and the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify facts about manipulated, response, and extraneous variables and the purposes of positive and negative controls; no wording, examples, tables, figures, or assay instructions were copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The accountable contributor checked the NCBI website and data usage policy before substantive access, used a small number of ordinary browser requests, and found no term prohibiting this limited agent reading. Source copyright and reuse were treated separately; lesson use is facts-only original expression.

claims.statement: In a supplied experiment, the deliberately manipulated factor is the independent variable, the recorded response is the dependent variable, and other relevant conditions deliberately held alike are controlled variables.
claims.scope: College-intro identification of roles within supplied experimental comparisons; detailed choices among experimental and observational designs are excluded.
claims.uncertainty: Terminology varies by field, and in observational or modelling contexts an independent variable may be an exposure, predictor, or explanatory variable rather than a researcher manipulation.

claims.statement: A factor creates confounding in a comparison when it varies systematically with the independent variable and can also influence the dependent variable, leaving the observed difference compatible with more than one explanation.
claims.scope: Introductory relational test for recognizing a candidate confounder in a supplied comparison.
claims.uncertainty: Whether a candidate factor actually affects the response requires subject-matter evidence; the role cannot be assigned from the factor name alone.

claims.statement: A negative-control condition provides an expected baseline or absence of the tested response, while a positive-control condition provides an expected response that checks whether the system can reveal the signal of interest.
claims.scope: Functional identification of positive and negative controls in bounded laboratory-like and engineering comparisons.
claims.uncertainty: The exact material, expected magnitude, and naming of controls depend on the field, system, and question.

claims.statement: Controlled variables and control conditions make particular sources of variation or system failure visible, but they do not eliminate every source of uncertainty or extend a conclusion beyond the conditions examined.
claims.scope: Introductory interpretation of supplied comparisons and control results.
claims.uncertainty: Which remaining uncertainties matter depends on the scientific system, measurements, sampling, and design.

terms.definition: A feature or quantity that can take different values or conditions in a comparison.
In the experiments used in this lesson, the factor deliberately changed between the main comparison conditions.
The response recorded to examine whether it differs across levels of the independent variable.
Another relevant condition deliberately kept alike across the main comparison so it does not create a systematic rival difference.
A factor that varies systematically with the independent variable and could also affect the dependent variable, creating a rival explanation for the observed difference.
An extra comparison condition included to provide a baseline, known response, or another specific check on interpretation.
A control condition expected to show the baseline or no tested response, used to reveal background signal or a response where none is expected.
A control condition expected to produce a known response, used to check that the system can reveal the signal of interest.

attributionHtml:
Attribution and provenance

Lesson identity

  • Lesson: PREM-SCI-003 — Variables and controls: what a comparison really tests
  • Version: 0.1.0
  • Outcome: topic-scientific-inquiry-variables-controls / PREM-02.03
  • Accountable principal: VSBDev (github:VSBDev)
  • Course-content licence: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International

Original contribution

The lesson prose, fictional cases, values, tables, prompts, feedback, assessment items, and glossary definitions were created for EmbeddedKnowledge and are contributed under CC BY 4.0 by the accountable principal. No third-party visual, media, dataset, quotation, or adapted expression is included.

The published PREM-SCI-001 learner-facing pack and the frozen PREM-SCI-002 candidate's learner-facing scenes, assessment, glossary, and metadata were consulted only for conceptual continuity. No review or adjudication artifact from either lesson was consulted, and no prior lesson prose, assessment, example, or structured artifact was copied into this pack.

Source use and rights

Two sources support factual and terminological claims. Both were used for facts only, followed by independent instructional structure and original expression. Complete locators, use records, rights evidence, and dated agent-access checks appear in references.json.

  • NIST/SEMATECH, e-Handbook of Statistical Methods: experimental factors, responses, controlled and uncontrolled inputs, and confounding.
  • Haas et al., In Vivo Assay Guidelines, NCBI Bookshelf / Assay Guidance Manual: manipulated, response, and extraneous variables and the functions of positive and negative controls.

Before substantive source access on 2026-07-20, the accountable run checked NIST's Copyrights & Disclaimers route and NCBI's Website and Data Usage Policies and Disclaimers. No term prohibiting the limited agent access used here was found. A separate OpenStax route was excluded after its current page notice identified a restriction on AI ingestion; it is not a lesson source and no OpenStax material was used.

Agent assistance disclosure

  • System: Codex
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: GPT-5
  • Version: Codex agent runtime 2026-07-20
  • Author run ID: 5619914e-ad81-4db9-8e6e-928a2f71146c
  • Material-instructions digest: sha256:8dec3af49e5263519344b46ec6b8542adb07e143432dbde96f13403875253357

The bounded one-pass final lesson revision and final accessibility-and-rights audit used Codex / OpenAI / GPT-5, version Codex agent runtime 2026-07-20, in fresh run 6a4a4597-b6c3-4f24-8b86-3bcd59d166ed. Its material-instructions digest is sha256:e60d304b1451b88b6133477d8d504f14d4f0fc0b98e6af6ca01a28ae4ceb9547; the separate adjudication.json records the finalizer's accountable decision provenance.

Each digest covers the exact UTF-8 material task payload supplied by the root agent, including its final newline. It excludes hidden provider and system instructions that cannot be exported. VSBDev remains accountable for the contribution and attests the disclosed provenance.

Assets and exceptions

No third-party assets are present. All learner operations have text-first paths; the tables are original semantic text representations rather than reused datasets or figures. There are no licence exceptions to the CC BY 4.0 contribution grant.
